items.title: Official ceremony for Notre Dame reopening following reconstruction | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The reopening service will take place in the presence of President Emmanuel Macron who will address numerous heads of state and government, dignitaries, patrons, and guests invited jointly by the State and the diocese, alongside representatives of all Parisian parishes, members of the cathedral chapter, and the clergy of Paris.

During the ritual of the opening of the doors, the Archbishop will strike the closed door of Notre-Dame de Paris with his crosier. The cathedral will “respond” by chanting Psalm 121 three times. After being silent for years, the cathedral will resound once again with songs of praise, and on the third chant, the doors will open.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Ghana elections dominated by concerns over worst economic crisis in a generation | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Polls have closed and vote-counting is underway in Ghana's presidential and parliamentary elections. The vote is largely a contest between the current Vice President Mahamudu Bawumia of the ruling New Patriotic Party and John Dramani Mahama of the main opposition National Democratic Congress Party. But many Ghanaians feel the leading candidates offer little hope for change, as the country struggles with its worst economic crisis in a generation.

Chapter Breakdown
0:00 Polls close in Ghana general election
0:30 Worst economic crisis in a generation overshadows elections
2:37 DW Correspondent Edith Kimani reports from Accra, Ghana

items.title: Syrian rebel leaders now say they control Homs as they eye Damascus | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syrian rebel leaders say they are now in full control of the strategic city of Homs after government forces withdrew. The city's capture leaves the road to Damascus open for the main rebel offensive from the north -- while opposition factions in the south are already on the capital's doorstep. 

These images on social media show Syrian protesters toppling a statue of the president's late father in the southern suburbs of Damascus. Syrian state media report that President Bashar al-Assad is still in the capital, although he hasn't been seen in days. Government soldiers have been seen fleeing across the border into Iraq.

In cities recently captured by the rebels, residents are already celebrating the end of Assad's decades-long rule.

Chapters:
0:00 Rebel leaders say they now control Homs
3:12 Aaron Zelin, Washington Institute for Near East Policy

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Notre Dame reopened in ceremony hosting dozens of dignitaries | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
A ceremony has been taking place in Paris to mark the reopening of Notre Dame Cathedral.

President Emmanuel Macron hosted dozens of world leaders and dignitaries in the French capital to celebrate the restoration. The cathedral in the heart of Paris has undergone a painstaking renovation to restore it to its former glory after a fire gutted its roof and woodwork in 2019, causing its famous spire to collapse. The main structure only narrowly avoided destruction.

items.title: Breaking: Assad flees Syria as rebel forces enter Damascus | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syrian President Bashar al-Assad has fled the capital Damascus to an unknown destination, after rebel forces entered the city to limited resistance.

The insurgents have declared the city free from Assad's decades-long rule, less than two weeks after launching their lightning offensive in the north of the country. Reuters reports that Syrian army command has notified its officers of the fall of the regime, citing senior military officials. Syria's Prime Minister Mohammed al- Jal-ali has announced he is ready to cooperate with a peaceful transfer of power to any leadership chosen by the people.  

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Assad flees Damascus: What to expect now in Syria | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
President Bashar al-Assad has reportedly fled the capital Damascus to an unknown destination, after rebel forces entered the city, meeting little resistance. 
The insurgents have declared the city free from Assad's rule that lasted for decades, less than two weeks after launching their lightning offensive. Citing senior military officials, Reuters reports that the Syrian army command has notified its officers of the fall of the regime. Syria's Prime Minister Mohammed al- Jal-ali has announced he is ready to cooperate in a peaceful transfer of power with any new leadership chosen by the people.

items.title: Assad flees: What it means for Syria and the wider region | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
In Syria, President Bashar al-Assad's regime has collapsed. Assad reportedly fled the capital Damascus to an unknown destination, after rebel forces entered the city, meeting little resistance. 
The insurgents have declared the city free from Assad's rule, which lasted 24 years. The rebels launched their offensive against the regime less than two weeks ago. Crowds have been celebrating in the capital and across Syria.
Turkey has taken more Syrian refugees than any other country -- and Syrians living in Turkey have been celebrating after rebel forces took control of Damascus and Bashar al-Assad fled the city.
In Istanbul, Syrians have been dancing, chanting and waving flags used by Syria's opposition to mark the rebels victory. Many Syrians in Turkey have said they could not return to their towns and villages while Assad remained in power. The United Nations says 3.6 million Syrians have been living in Turkey since the Syrian war broke out in 2011.

00:00 Assad flees
03:38 DW speaks with Fawaz Gerges, Professor of Middle Eastern Political and International Relations at the London School of Economics 
10:46 DW's Gasia Ohanes reports from the Studio in Berlin
18:40 DW's Dorian Jones reports from Istanbul


For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: South Korea's Yoon Suk-yeol narrowly avoids impeachment | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
South Korea's President Yoon Suk-yeol narrowly avoided impeachment after members of his own party boycotted the vote. Outside parliament, angry protesters vowed to keep fighting as opposition parties pledged another attempt to oust him, deepening the country’s political crisis.

items.title: Syrians in neighboring Lebanon hopeful to return home despite uncertainty ahead | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The fall of Bashar al-Assad’s regime marks a historic day for Syrians living abroad. Many Syrians living in Lebanon have expressed cautious optimism that the end of the Assad family's more than 50-year rule will make way for a better chapter. In the meantime, Lebanon has sent army reinforcements to its border with Syria. DW’s Mohamad Chreyteh travelled to the Lebanese-Syrian border and asked Syrian refugees about their hopes and concerns for the future.

items.title: Who is the leader of Syrian Islamist group HTS, and how is the region reacting to Assad's downfall?
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The Islamist rebel group Hayat Tahrir al-Sham, or HTS, led by Abu Mohammad al-Jolani, has toppled the regime of Syria’s President Bashar al-Assad. The group insists it has renounced its ties to al-Qaeda and that it will respect pluralism and the rights of minorities. Since the downfall of Assad’s regime, the Syrian President has reportedly fled the country and been granted asylum in Russia. 
Meanwhile Israel has seized a buffer zone in the Syrian-controlled area of the Golan Heights. It says this is a temporary but necessary measure since Syrian soldiers had abandoned their positions. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has said their departure means Israel's 1974 ceasefire agreement with Syria has collapsed, and Israeli forces must now take over the territory to protect against any potential threats.

Chapter Breakdown
00:00 Anas Salkhadi, HTS rebel commander, says "Syria is for everyone"
00:34 Rebels loot presidential palace in Damascus
1:03 Hans-Jakob Schindler, Senior Director at the Counter Extremism Project, on Syrian Islamist group Hayat Tahrir al-Sham

items.title: Germany welcomes Assad's downfall, offers Syria assistance to rebuild | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
German Chancellor Olaf Scholz has welcomed the fall of the Syrian Assad regime and offered Berlin's help to stabilize the war-torn country. 
Syrians in Berlin took to the streets on Sunday to celebrate, waving flags used by the Syrian opposition. Germany is home to nearly a million Syrians who fled the civil war in their home country in 2015. Most of them were granted asylum protection and given the right to live and work in Germany. 

Chapter Breakdown
0:00 Syrians in Berlin celebrate fall of Assad regime
00:47 German Chancellor Olaf Scholz gives statement welcoming fall of Assad regime
2:47 DW's Chief Political Editor, Michael Küfner, on how Germany's political parties might react to a new Syrian regime
